aiMessages.content: Create a telegram bot that shares crypto updates from x.com, the updates can be from any account so far as it has over 3k views.

Implemented the Telegram repost bot with MongoDB persistence, admin commands for accounts/destinations/threshold/keywords, an X client that calls CookMyBots X Proxy, and a safe polling loop that deduplicates per destination and skips when X integration is not configured. Added startup diagnostics, DOCS.md, README.md, and a .env.sample with the required keys.

planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Crypto Updates Bot (X.com view-threshold aggregator)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that curates and forwards crypto-related updates from X.com into Telegram chats/channels. The bot ingests posts from a configurable set of X.com accounts, applies a minimum popularity threshold (over 3k views), optionally performs basic filtering/deduplication, and publishes formatted updates on a schedule or near-real-time.

The system runs as a single Node.js (ESM) process using grammY for Telegram integration, with MongoDB for persistence. It supports admin workflows to manage tracked accounts, destination chats, filters, and posting cadence; and includes guardrails for rate limits, deduplication, operational monitoring, and safe failure modes.

Product goals:
Primary outcomes
1) Deliver timely crypto updates into Telegram with minimal noise by requiring posts to exceed a view threshold (default: 3,000 views).
2) Allow admins to configure which X.com accounts are monitored and where updates are published (private groups, public channels, or direct messages).
3) Ensure updates are reliably deduplicated and posted with consistent formatting and attribution.
4) Provide operational controls: pause/resume, backfill window, posting frequency, per-account toggles, and basic keyword filtering.

Target users and roles
1) Admins (operators): configure sources, destinations, filters, and moderation settings; manage allowlists/denylists; review status.
2) Readers: consume updates in Telegram; optionally request latest items on demand.

Success criteria
1) High signal-to-noise: most forwarded posts are relevant and meet the view threshold.
2) Low duplication: the same X post is not repeatedly forwarded.
3) Operational stability: bot continues to function under API limits and transient errors, with clear status reporting.

Non-goals (initial version)
1) Complex NLP summarization, sentiment, or trading signals.
2) Multi-process scaling or external queues.
3) Full-text search across all historical posts (unless later added via MongoDB indexes).

High-level architecture:
High-level components (single Node.js service)
1) Telegram Bot Interface (grammY)
   1) Handles commands, admin configuration flows, and status queries.
   2) Sends messages to configured Telegram destinations.

2) X.com Ingestion Layer
   1) Responsible for retrieving candidate posts from configured accounts.
   2) Normalizes post metadata into an internal representation (id, author, text, url, createdat, viewcount if available, media references).
   3) Runs on a schedule (polling) and supports a controlled backfill window.

3) Filtering and Eligibility Engine
   1) Applies global and per-destination filters (minimum view threshold, keyword include/exclude, language constraints, allow/deny accounts).
   2) Enforces deduplication using a persistent record of posted X ids per destination.
   3) Applies content safety policies (e.g., block terms or domains) as configured.

4) Publishing and Formatting
   1) Composes Telegram-friendly messages (author, truncated text, link, view count, timestamp).
   2) Supports destination-specific templates (e.g., include hashtags, add source label).
   3) Handles media attachments if desired (optional, depends on ingestion metadata availability).

5) Persistence (MongoDB)
   1) Stores configuration, tracked accounts, destinations, posting state (cursors/last seen), dedup history, and audit logs.

6) Scheduler and Rate Control
   1) A simple internal scheduler runs periodic ingestion cycles.
   2) Concurrency and throughput limits prevent hitting Telegram and X rate limits.

7) Observability and Operations
   1) Structured logs for ingestion cycles, posting decisions, and errors.
   2) Health indicators (last successful run, backlog size, last posted time).
   3) Admin-facing /status that reflects current state and recent failures.

Execution model
1) Poll-based ingestion: every N seconds/minutes, fetch recent posts from each tracked account.
2) For each candidate post: compute eligibility, check dedup, then enqueue for sending within the same process using an in-memory buffer with strict concurrency limits.
3) Persist a minimal state after each cycle so restarts do not repost old items.

External dependency note
1) Accessing X.com content must be done via an approved method available to the project (e.g., an API integration or permitted fetching mechanism). The architecture treats it as an external provider and isolates it behind a single adapter so it can be swapped without impacting bot logic.
2) View-count availability depends on the provider. If view count is not available, the system can operate in a degraded mode using alternative signals (likes/retweets) or skip eligibility checks; this is configurable and clearly surfaced in /status.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (suggested)

1) users
Purpose: Telegram user records for admins/operators and optionally readers.
Key fields
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) role: admin | viewer
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) preferences (optional): language, timezone

2) destinations
Purpose: Telegram chats/channels where updates are published.
Key fields
1) destinationId: Telegram chat id (unique)
2) type: privatechat | group | supergroup | channel
3) title (cached)
4) enabled: boolean
5) settings
   1) minViews (default 3000)
   2) postIntervalSeconds (rate throttling per destination)
   3) includeKeywords (array)
   4) excludeKeywords (array)
   5) blockedDomains (array)
   6) templateMode (e.g., compact | detailed)
6) createdAt, updatedAt

3) sources
Purpose: Tracked X.com accounts.
Key fields
1) sourceId (unique, internal)
2) xHandle (unique)
3) displayName (cached)
4) enabled: boolean
5) tags (e.g., influencer, news, project)
6) createdAt, updatedAt

4) subscriptions
Purpose: Mapping from sources to destinations (which destinations receive which sources).
Key fields
1) destinationId
2) sourceId
3) enabled: boolean
4) perSubscriptionOverrides (optional)
   1) minViews override
   2) include/exclude keywords override
5) createdAt, updatedAt
Indexes: compound unique on (destinationId, sourceId)

5) ingestionstate
Purpose: Track polling progress per source to avoid reprocessing.
Key fields
1) sourceId (unique)
2) lastSeenPostId or lastSeenTimestamp (depending on provider)
3) lastRunAt
4) lastSuccessAt
5) failureCount, lastError

6) posts
Purpose: Store normalized posts and eligibility decisions for auditing and dedup.
Key fields
1) xPostId (unique across all)
2) sourceId
3) url
4) text (truncated or full, depending on storage policy)
5) createdAt (post time)
6) metrics
   1) views
   2) likes
   3) reposts
7) mediaRefs (optional): array of media urls/ids if available
8) ingestedAt
Indexes: xPostId unique, sourceId+createdAt

7) deliveries
Purpose: Track what was sent to which destination to prevent duplicates.
Key fields
1) xPostId
2) destinationId
3) telegramMessageId (if sent)
4) status: sent | skipped | failed
5) reason (if skipped/failed): belowthreshold, keywordfiltered, duplicate, error
6) sentAt
Indexes: compound unique on (xPostId, destinationId)

8) auditlogs
Purpose: Admin actions and configuration changes.
Key fields
1) actorTelegramUserId
2) actionType
3) entityType, entityId
4) before, after (optional snapshots)
5) createdAt

Retention policies
1) posts: retain 30-180 days depending on storage and audit needs.
2) deliveries: retain longer for dedup (e.g., 180-365 days) with indexes for fast lookups.
3) auditlogs: retain according to operational requirements.

Key flows:
1) Initial setup (admin)
1) Admin starts bot and is recognized as admin via a configured allowlist (environment-based) or a one-time manual approval process stored in MongoDB.
2) Admin runs /setup to create the first destination and confirm the bot has permission to post.
3) Admin adds sources via /sourceadd and subscribes destinations via /subscribe.
4) Admin sets thresholds and filters globally and per destination.

2) Ingestion cycle (polling)
1) Scheduler triggers a cycle every configured interval.
2) For each enabled source:
   1) Fetch latest posts since ingestionstate cursor.
   2) Normalize items; store/merge into posts.
   3) Update ingestionstate on success.
3) For each candidate post:
   1) Determine interested destinations via subscriptions.
   2) For each destination:
      1) Check deliveries for dedup.
      2) Apply eligibility rules: view threshold (minViews), keyword filters, blocked domains.
      3) If eligible, create a pending send task in an in-memory buffer.

3) Publishing
1) Send tasks are processed with strict concurrency limits (global and per destination).
2) For each send:
   1) Render message per destination template.
   2) Send via grammY to the destination.
   3) Record delivery with telegramMessageId and status sent.
3) On failure:
   1) Record delivery status failed with reason.
   2) Apply retry policy for transient errors with capped attempts in-memory; avoid infinite retries across restarts.

4) Manual pull by users
1) /latest returns the most recent eligible posts for a destination (or via DM) without posting them to channels, depending on permissions.
2) /search (optional) allows admins to query stored posts by handle/keyword/time window for debugging.

5) Moderation and safety controls
1) /pause stops publishing but can continue ingestion (configurable) to keep state warm.
2) /resume re-enables publishing.
3) /blockkeyword and /blockdomain immediately prevent future deliveries matching these rules.
4) /sourcedisable quickly stops monitoring a problematic account.

6) Restart and recovery
1) On restart, bot loads configuration from MongoDB.
2) Scheduler resumes ingestion using stored cursors.
3) Dedup continues to work because deliveries are persisted.
4) If MongoDB is temporarily unavailable, bot enters a safe mode: no publishing, only minimal status reporting, and retries until storage returns.

Tech stack:
Runtime and structure
1) Node.js (ESM) single-process service.
2) grammY for Telegram bot handling, including command routing, middleware, and message sending.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for configuration, state, deduplication, and audit logs.
2) In-memory maps for ephemeral runtime concerns only (in-flight tasks, short-lived caches, rate-limit counters), always safe to lose.

External integrations
1) X.com data access via a single adapter module that abstracts the chosen retrieval method.
2) Object storage or simple filesystem storage only if media caching or archival is required (optional). If used, store only derived artifacts and keep source-of-truth in MongoDB.

Operations
1) Environment variables for secrets and configuration, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.
2) Structured logging to stdout/stderr.
3) A simple health endpoint is optional if the environment supports HTTP within the same Node.js process; otherwise, use /status for health visibility.

Deployment
1) Deploy as one Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Configure process restart on crash at the hosting layer.
3) Use a single MongoDB instance/cluster already integrated with the project.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) At-least-once ingestion, exactly-once delivery per destination achieved via deliveries unique index (xPostId + destinationId).
2) Safe restarts: ingestion cursors and dedup history are persisted.
3) Backpressure: bounded in-memory send queue; if exceeded, the bot skips or delays low-priority sends and reports via /status.

Rate limiting and throughput
1) Apply conservative per-destination message throttling and a global concurrency cap.
2) X.com fetching uses a per-source schedule and caps requests per cycle.
3) Use incremental polling with cursors to reduce load.

Security
1) Admin access control: only approved Telegram user ids can change configuration.
2) Secret management via environment variables.
3) Input validation for commands to prevent misconfiguration.

Observability
1) Log each ingestion cycle summary: sources processed, candidates found, eligible count, sent count, skipped reasons.
2) Log each failure with source, destination, error category.
3) /status provides: enabled sources, enabled destinations, last run timestamps, last error, queue depth, and current thresholds.

Maintainability
1) Clear modular boundaries: Telegram interface, ingestion adapter, eligibility engine, publisher, persistence layer.
2) Provider adapter designed to be replaceable without changes to core logic.

Compliance and content considerations
1) Respect the data provider’s terms and applicable restrictions.
2) Provide attribution and link back to the original post.
3) Offer an admin-controlled denylist for accounts and keywords to reduce scams/spam.

Testing strategy (high level)
1) Unit-test eligibility logic and formatting using stored fixtures.
2) Integration-test MongoDB persistence and dedup behavior.
3) End-to-end smoke test in a private Telegram chat before enabling channel posting.

Bot commands:
• /start — Initialize the bot and show basic help plus current status for the user. (usage: /start) [general]
• /help — Show available commands and usage, tailored to admin vs non-admin. (usage: /help) [general]
• /status — Show operational status: sources, destinations, last ingestion run, last error, queue depth, and current thresholds. (usage: /status) [operations]
• /setup — Guide an admin through initial setup: register a destination chat/channel and default settings. (usage: /setup) [admin]
• /destadd — Register the current chat as a destination for postings (admin only). (usage: /destadd) [admin]
• /destlist — List all configured destinations and key settings. (usage: /destlist) [admin]
• /destenable — Enable publishing to a destination. (usage: /destenable <destinationId>) [admin]
• /destdisable — Disable publishing to a destination without deleting configuration. (usage: /destdisable <destinationId>) [admin]
• /sourceadd — Add an X.com account to monitor. (usage: /sourceadd <xHandle>) [admin]
• /sourcelist — List monitored X.com accounts and whether they are enabled. (usage: /sourcelist) [admin]
• /sourceenable — Enable monitoring for an account. (usage: /sourceenable <xHandle>) [admin]
• /sourcedisable — Disable monitoring for an account. (usage: /sourcedisable <xHandle>) [admin]
• /subscribe — Subscribe a destination to a source (start forwarding eligible posts). (usage: /subscribe <destinationId> <xHandle>) [admin]
• /unsubscribe — Remove a subscription between destination and source. (usage: /unsubscribe <destinationId> <xHandle>) [admin]
• /setminviews — Set the minimum view threshold globally or per destination (default 3000). (usage: /setminviews <views> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /setinterval — Set ingestion polling interval and/or per-destination posting throttle. (usage: /setinterval <seconds> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /filterinclude — Set or add include keywords for eligibility (global or per destination). (usage: /filterinclude <keyword1,keyword2,...> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /filterexclude — Set or add exclude keywords for eligibility (global or per destination). (usage: /filterexclude <keyword1,keyword2,...> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /blockdomain — Block posts containing links to specific domains (global or per destination). (usage: /blockdomain <domain> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /pause — Pause publishing (optionally continue ingestion) for maintenance or incident response. (usage: /pause) [operations]
• /resume — Resume publishing after a pause. (usage: /resume) [operations]
• /latest — Show the most recent eligible posts for a destination or source without altering delivery state (useful for spot checks). (usage: /latest [xHandle] [limit]) [general]
• /backfill — Trigger a controlled backfill for the last N hours for one source or all sources (admin only). (usage: /backfill <hours> [xHandle]) [admin]

Testing strategy (high level)

2) Integration-test MongoDB persistence and dedup behavior.
3) End-to-end smoke test in a private Telegram chat before enabling channel posting.

DOCS.md.content:
This Telegram bot monitors selected X accounts and reposts qualifying posts into Telegram when the post has enough views.

How it works
1) You add one or more X accounts (handles).
2) You mark one or more Telegram chats as destinations.
3) The bot polls X periodically.
4) If a new post meets the minimum views threshold (default 3000) and passes keyword filters, it gets forwarded to all destinations.
5) The bot deduplicates per destination so the same X post is never sent twice to the same chat.

Public commands
1) /start
Shows what the bot does and quick setup guidance.

2) /help
Shows the full command list and troubleshooting.

3) /status
Shows the bot status (polling interval, number of accounts, number of destinations, last poll time, whether X integration is enabled).

Admin commands
Admin commands require your Telegram user ID to be listed in ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS.

1) /addaccount <handleOrUrl>
Adds an X account to monitor.
Example: /addaccount @binance
Example: /addaccount https://x.com/binance

2) /removeaccount <handleOrUrl>
Removes an X account.

3) /accounts
Lists monitored accounts.

4) /setdest
Run this in the chat you want to receive updates. The bot will save the current chat as a destination.

5) /removedest
Run this in a destination chat to remove it.

6) /dests
Lists destination chats.

7) /setthreshold <number>
Sets the minimum views threshold. It is always clamped to at least 3000.

8) /setkeywords include <comma-separated>
Sets include keywords. If include keywords are present, a post must contain at least one.
Example: /setkeywords include btc,eth,sol

9) /setkeywords exclude <comma-separated>
Sets exclude keywords. If a post contains any excluded keyword, it is skipped.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Required. Telegram token from BotFather.

2) MONGODB_URI
Required. Mongo connection string.

3) COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT
Optional but required for polling X. If missing, the bot still starts but polling is skipped.

4) COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY
Optional but required for polling X.

5) ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS
Required for admin commands. Comma-separated Telegram user IDs.
If missing, the bot will report that no admins are configured.

6) POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS
Optional. Defaults to 120.

Collections used (MongoDB)
1) settings
Stores threshold and keyword filters.

2) accounts
Monitored X accounts.

3) destinations
Telegram chats where updates are posted. The bot can auto-disable a destination after repeated send failures.

4) sent_posts
Deduplication records keyed by (destinationChatId, xPostId).

5) last_seen
Per-account cursor to reduce reprocessing.

Troubleshooting
1) The bot is not reposting anything
Check /status. If X integration is disabled, you need COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY.
Also ensure you have at least one account and at least one destination.

2) Admin commands say no admins configured
Set ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS to your Telegram numeric user ID (comma-separated if multiple), then redeploy.

3) Messages fail to send to a destination
The bot will log send errors and may auto-disable the destination after several failures. Re-add the destination using /setdest after fixing permissions.

README.md.content:
Telegram bot that monitors configured X accounts and reposts qualifying posts to Telegram when they have enough views.

Setup
1) Create a bot with BotFather and set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN.
2) Create a MongoDB database and set MONGODB_URI.
3) Configure the CookMyBots X integration and set COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY.
4) Set ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS to your Telegram numeric user id.
5) Start the bot.

Run
1) npm run dev
2) npm start

Basic usage
1) In the destination chat, run /setdest
2) In any chat where you are admin, run /addaccount <handle>
3) Wait for polling, or check /status

Notes
This bot uses long polling for Telegram updates and runs in a single Node.js process.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Crypto Updates Bot (X.com view-threshold aggregator)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that curates and forwards crypto-related updates from X.com into Telegram chats/channels. The bot ingests posts from a configurable set of X.com accounts, applies a minimum popularity threshold (over 3k views), optionally performs basic filtering/deduplication, and publishes formatted updates on a schedule or near-real-time.

The system runs as a single Node.js (ESM) process using grammY for Telegram integration, with MongoDB for persistence. It supports admin workflows to manage tracked accounts, destination chats, filters, and posting cadence; and includes guardrails for rate limits, deduplication, operational monitoring, and safe failure modes.

Product goals:
Primary outcomes
1) Deliver timely crypto updates into Telegram with minimal noise by requiring posts to exceed a view threshold (default: 3,000 views).
2) Allow admins to configure which X.com accounts are monitored and where updates are published (private groups, public channels, or direct messages).
3) Ensure updates are reliably deduplicated and posted with consistent formatting and attribution.
4) Provide operational controls: pause/resume, backfill window, posting frequency, per-account toggles, and basic keyword filtering.

Target users and roles
1) Admins (operators): configure sources, destinations, filters, and moderation settings; manage allowlists/denylists; review status.
2) Readers: consume updates in Telegram; optionally request latest items on demand.

Success criteria
1) High signal-to-noise: most forwarded posts are relevant and meet the view threshold.
2) Low duplication: the same X post is not repeatedly forwarded.
3) Operational stability: bot continues to function under API limits and transient errors, with clear status reporting.

Non-goals (initial version)
1) Complex NLP summarization, sentiment, or trading signals.
2) Multi-process scaling or external queues.
3) Full-text search across all historical posts (unless later added via MongoDB indexes).

High-level architecture:
High-level components (single Node.js service)
1) Telegram Bot Interface (grammY)
   1) Handles commands, admin configuration flows, and status queries.
   2) Sends messages to configured Telegram destinations.

2) X.com Ingestion Layer
   1) Responsible for retrieving candidate posts from configured accounts.
   2) Normalizes post metadata into an internal representation (id, author, text, url, createdat, viewcount if available, media references).
   3) Runs on a schedule (polling) and supports a controlled backfill window.

3) Filtering and Eligibility Engine
   1) Applies global and per-destination filters (minimum view threshold, keyword include/exclude, language constraints, allow/deny accounts).
   2) Enforces deduplication using a persistent record of posted X ids per destination.
   3) Applies content safety policies (e.g., block terms or domains) as configured.

4) Publishing and Formatting
   1) Composes Telegram-friendly messages (author, truncated text, link, view count, timestamp).
   2) Supports destination-specific templates (e.g., include hashtags, add source label).
   3) Handles media attachments if desired (optional, depends on ingestion metadata availability).

5) Persistence (MongoDB)
   1) Stores configuration, tracked accounts, destinations, posting state (cursors/last seen), dedup history, and audit logs.

6) Scheduler and Rate Control
   1) A simple internal scheduler runs periodic ingestion cycles.
   2) Concurrency and throughput limits prevent hitting Telegram and X rate limits.

7) Observability and Operations
   1) Structured logs for ingestion cycles, posting decisions, and errors.
   2) Health indicators (last successful run, backlog size, last posted time).
   3) Admin-facing /status that reflects current state and recent failures.

Execution model
1) Poll-based ingestion: every N seconds/minutes, fetch recent posts from each tracked account.
2) For each candidate post: compute eligibility, check dedup, then enqueue for sending within the same process using an in-memory buffer with strict concurrency limits.
3) Persist a minimal state after each cycle so restarts do not repost old items.

External dependency note
1) Accessing X.com content must be done via an approved method available to the project (e.g., an API integration or permitted fetching mechanism). The architecture treats it as an external provider and isolates it behind a single adapter so it can be swapped without impacting bot logic.
2) View-count availability depends on the provider. If view count is not available, the system can operate in a degraded mode using alternative signals (likes/retweets) or skip eligibility checks; this is configurable and clearly surfaced in /status.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (suggested)

1) users
Purpose: Telegram user records for admins/operators and optionally readers.
Key fields
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) role: admin | viewer
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) preferences (optional): language, timezone

2) destinations
Purpose: Telegram chats/channels where updates are published.
Key fields
1) destinationId: Telegram chat id (unique)
2) type: privatechat | group | supergroup | channel
3) title (cached)
4) enabled: boolean
5) settings
   1) minViews (default 3000)
   2) postIntervalSeconds (rate throttling per destination)
   3) includeKeywords (array)
   4) excludeKeywords (array)
   5) blockedDomains (array)
   6) templateMode (e.g., compact | detailed)
6) createdAt, updatedAt

3) sources
Purpose: Tracked X.com accounts.
Key fields
1) sourceId (unique, internal)
2) xHandle (unique)
3) displayName (cached)
4) enabled: boolean
5) tags (e.g., influencer, news, project)
6) createdAt, updatedAt

4) subscriptions
Purpose: Mapping from sources to destinations (which destinations receive which sources).
Key fields
1) destinationId
2) sourceId
3) enabled: boolean
4) perSubscriptionOverrides (optional)
   1) minViews override
   2) include/exclude keywords override
5) createdAt, updatedAt
Indexes: compound unique on (destinationId, sourceId)

5) ingestionstate
Purpose: Track polling progress per source to avoid reprocessing.
Key fields
1) sourceId (unique)
2) lastSeenPostId or lastSeenTimestamp (depending on provider)
3) lastRunAt
4) lastSuccessAt
5) failureCount, lastError

6) posts
Purpose: Store normalized posts and eligibility decisions for auditing and dedup.
Key fields
1) xPostId (unique across all)
2) sourceId
3) url
4) text (truncated or full, depending on storage policy)
5) createdAt (post time)
6) metrics
   1) views
   2) likes
   3) reposts
7) mediaRefs (optional): array of media urls/ids if available
8) ingestedAt
Indexes: xPostId unique, sourceId+createdAt

7) deliveries
Purpose: Track what was sent to which destination to prevent duplicates.
Key fields
1) xPostId
2) destinationId
3) telegramMessageId (if sent)
4) status: sent | skipped | failed
5) reason (if skipped/failed): belowthreshold, keywordfiltered, duplicate, error
6) sentAt
Indexes: compound unique on (xPostId, destinationId)

8) auditlogs
Purpose: Admin actions and configuration changes.
Key fields
1) actorTelegramUserId
2) actionType
3) entityType, entityId
4) before, after (optional snapshots)
5) createdAt

Retention policies
1) posts: retain 30-180 days depending on storage and audit needs.
2) deliveries: retain longer for dedup (e.g., 180-365 days) with indexes for fast lookups.
3) auditlogs: retain according to operational requirements.

Key flows:
1) Initial setup (admin)
1) Admin starts bot and is recognized as admin via a configured allowlist (environment-based) or a one-time manual approval process stored in MongoDB.
2) Admin runs /setup to create the first destination and confirm the bot has permission to post.
3) Admin adds sources via /sourceadd and subscribes destinations via /subscribe.
4) Admin sets thresholds and filters globally and per destination.

2) Ingestion cycle (polling)
1) Scheduler triggers a cycle every configured interval.
2) For each enabled source:
   1) Fetch latest posts since ingestionstate cursor.
   2) Normalize items; store/merge into posts.
   3) Update ingestionstate on success.
3) For each candidate post:
   1) Determine interested destinations via subscriptions.
   2) For each destination:
      1) Check deliveries for dedup.
      2) Apply eligibility rules: view threshold (minViews), keyword filters, blocked domains.
      3) If eligible, create a pending send task in an in-memory buffer.

3) Publishing
1) Send tasks are processed with strict concurrency limits (global and per destination).
2) For each send:
   1) Render message per destination template.
   2) Send via grammY to the destination.
   3) Record delivery with telegramMessageId and status sent.
3) On failure:
   1) Record delivery status failed with reason.
   2) Apply retry policy for transient errors with capped attempts in-memory; avoid infinite retries across restarts.

4) Manual pull by users
1) /latest returns the most recent eligible posts for a destination (or via DM) without posting them to channels, depending on permissions.
2) /search (optional) allows admins to query stored posts by handle/keyword/time window for debugging.

5) Moderation and safety controls
1) /pause stops publishing but can continue ingestion (configurable) to keep state warm.
2) /resume re-enables publishing.
3) /blockkeyword and /blockdomain immediately prevent future deliveries matching these rules.
4) /sourcedisable quickly stops monitoring a problematic account.

6) Restart and recovery
1) On restart, bot loads configuration from MongoDB.
2) Scheduler resumes ingestion using stored cursors.
3) Dedup continues to work because deliveries are persisted.
4) If MongoDB is temporarily unavailable, bot enters a safe mode: no publishing, only minimal status reporting, and retries until storage returns.

Tech stack:
Runtime and structure
1) Node.js (ESM) single-process service.
2) grammY for Telegram bot handling, including command routing, middleware, and message sending.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for configuration, state, deduplication, and audit logs.
2) In-memory maps for ephemeral runtime concerns only (in-flight tasks, short-lived caches, rate-limit counters), always safe to lose.

External integrations
1) X.com data access via a single adapter module that abstracts the chosen retrieval method.
2) Object storage or simple filesystem storage only if media caching or archival is required (optional). If used, store only derived artifacts and keep source-of-truth in MongoDB.

Operations
1) Environment variables for secrets and configuration, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.
2) Structured logging to stdout/stderr.
3) A simple health endpoint is optional if the environment supports HTTP within the same Node.js process; otherwise, use /status for health visibility.

Deployment
1) Deploy as one Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Configure process restart on crash at the hosting layer.
3) Use a single MongoDB instance/cluster already integrated with the project.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) At-least-once ingestion, exactly-once delivery per destination achieved via deliveries unique index (xPostId + destinationId).
2) Safe restarts: ingestion cursors and dedup history are persisted.
3) Backpressure: bounded in-memory send queue; if exceeded, the bot skips or delays low-priority sends and reports via /status.

Rate limiting and throughput
1) Apply conservative per-destination message throttling and a global concurrency cap.
2) X.com fetching uses a per-source schedule and caps requests per cycle.
3) Use incremental polling with cursors to reduce load.

Security
1) Admin access control: only approved Telegram user ids can change configuration.
2) Secret management via environment variables.
3) Input validation for commands to prevent misconfiguration.

Observability
1) Log each ingestion cycle summary: sources processed, candidates found, eligible count, sent count, skipped reasons.
2) Log each failure with source, destination, error category.
3) /status provides: enabled sources, enabled destinations, last run timestamps, last error, queue depth, and current thresholds.

Maintainability
1) Clear modular boundaries: Telegram interface, ingestion adapter, eligibility engine, publisher, persistence layer.
2) Provider adapter designed to be replaceable without changes to core logic.

Compliance and content considerations
1) Respect the data provider’s terms and applicable restrictions.
2) Provide attribution and link back to the original post.
3) Offer an admin-controlled denylist for accounts and keywords to reduce scams/spam.

Testing strategy (high level)
1) Unit-test eligibility logic and formatting using stored fixtures.
2) Integration-test MongoDB persistence and dedup behavior.
3) End-to-end smoke test in a private Telegram chat before enabling channel posting.

Bot commands:
• /start — Initialize the bot and show basic help plus current status for the user. (usage: /start) [general]
• /help — Show available commands and usage, tailored to admin vs non-admin. (usage: /help) [general]
• /status — Show operational status: sources, destinations, last ingestion run, last error, queue depth, and current thresholds. (usage: /status) [operations]
• /setup — Guide an admin through initial setup: register a destination chat/channel and default settings. (usage: /setup) [admin]
• /destadd — Register the current chat as a destination for postings (admin only). (usage: /destadd) [admin]
• /destlist — List all configured destinations and key settings. (usage: /destlist) [admin]
• /destenable — Enable publishing to a destination. (usage: /destenable <destinationId>) [admin]
• /destdisable — Disable publishing to a destination without deleting configuration. (usage: /destdisable <destinationId>) [admin]
• /sourceadd — Add an X.com account to monitor. (usage: /sourceadd <xHandle>) [admin]
• /sourcelist — List monitored X.com accounts and whether they are enabled. (usage: /sourcelist) [admin]
• /sourceenable — Enable monitoring for an account. (usage: /sourceenable <xHandle>) [admin]
• /sourcedisable — Disable monitoring for an account. (usage: /sourcedisable <xHandle>) [admin]
• /subscribe — Subscribe a destination to a source (start forwarding eligible posts). (usage: /subscribe <destinationId> <xHandle>) [admin]
• /unsubscribe — Remove a subscription between destination and source. (usage: /unsubscribe <destinationId> <xHandle>) [admin]
• /setminviews — Set the minimum view threshold globally or per destination (default 3000). (usage: /setminviews <views> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /setinterval — Set ingestion polling interval and/or per-destination posting throttle. (usage: /setinterval <seconds> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /filterinclude — Set or add include keywords for eligibility (global or per destination). (usage: /filterinclude <keyword1,keyword2,...> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /filterexclude — Set or add exclude keywords for eligibility (global or per destination). (usage: /filterexclude <keyword1,keyword2,...> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /blockdomain — Block posts containing links to specific domains (global or per destination). (usage: /blockdomain <domain> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /pause — Pause publishing (optionally continue ingestion) for maintenance or incident response. (usage: /pause) [operations]
• /resume — Resume publishing after a pause. (usage: /resume) [operations]
• /latest — Show the most recent eligible posts for a destination or source without altering delivery state (useful for spot checks). (usage: /latest [xHandle] [limit]) [general]
• /backfill — Trigger a controlled backfill for the last N hours for one source or all sources (admin only). (usage: /backfill <hours> [xHandle]) [admin]

planSections.productGoals:
Primary outcomes
1) Deliver timely crypto updates into Telegram with minimal noise by requiring posts to exceed a view threshold (default: 3,000 views).
2) Allow admins to configure which X.com accounts are monitored and where updates are published (private groups, public channels, or direct messages).
3) Ensure updates are reliably deduplicated and posted with consistent formatting and attribution.
4) Provide operational controls: pause/resume, backfill window, posting frequency, per-account toggles, and basic keyword filtering.

Target users and roles
1) Admins (operators): configure sources, destinations, filters, and moderation settings; manage allowlists/denylists; review status.
2) Readers: consume updates in Telegram; optionally request latest items on demand.

Success criteria
1) High signal-to-noise: most forwarded posts are relevant and meet the view threshold.
2) Low duplication: the same X post is not repeatedly forwarded.
3) Operational stability: bot continues to function under API limits and transient errors, with clear status reporting.

Non-goals (initial version)
1) Complex NLP summarization, sentiment, or trading signals.
2) Multi-process scaling or external queues.
3) Full-text search across all historical posts (unless later added via MongoDB indexes).
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
High-level components (single Node.js service)
1) Telegram Bot Interface (grammY)
   1) Handles commands, admin configuration flows, and status queries.
   2) Sends messages to configured Telegram destinations.

2) X.com Ingestion Layer
   1) Responsible for retrieving candidate posts from configured accounts.
   2) Normalizes post metadata into an internal representation (id, author, text, url, createdat, viewcount if available, media references).
   3) Runs on a schedule (polling) and supports a controlled backfill window.

3) Filtering and Eligibility Engine
   1) Applies global and per-destination filters (minimum view threshold, keyword include/exclude, language constraints, allow/deny accounts).
   2) Enforces deduplication using a persistent record of posted X ids per destination.
   3) Applies content safety policies (e.g., block terms or domains) as configured.

4) Publishing and Formatting
   1) Composes Telegram-friendly messages (author, truncated text, link, view count, timestamp).
   2) Supports destination-specific templates (e.g., include hashtags, add source label).
   3) Handles media attachments if desired (optional, depends on ingestion metadata availability).

5) Persistence (MongoDB)
   1) Stores configuration, tracked accounts, destinations, posting state (cursors/last seen), dedup history, and audit logs.

6) Scheduler and Rate Control
   1) A simple internal scheduler runs periodic ingestion cycles.
   2) Concurrency and throughput limits prevent hitting Telegram and X rate limits.

7) Observability and Operations
   1) Structured logs for ingestion cycles, posting decisions, and errors.
   2) Health indicators (last successful run, backlog size, last posted time).
   3) Admin-facing /status that reflects current state and recent failures.

Execution model
1) Poll-based ingestion: every N seconds/minutes, fetch recent posts from each tracked account.
2) For each candidate post: compute eligibility, check dedup, then enqueue for sending within the same process using an in-memory buffer with strict concurrency limits.
3) Persist a minimal state after each cycle so restarts do not repost old items.

External dependency note
1) Accessing X.com content must be done via an approved method available to the project (e.g., an API integration or permitted fetching mechanism). The architecture treats it as an external provider and isolates it behind a single adapter so it can be swapped without impacting bot logic.
2) View-count availability depends on the provider. If view count is not available, the system can operate in a degraded mode using alternative signals (likes/retweets) or skip eligibility checks; this is configurable and clearly surfaced in /status.
planSections.dataModel:
MongoDB collections (suggested)

1) users
Purpose: Telegram user records for admins/operators and optionally readers.
Key fields
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) role: admin | viewer
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) preferences (optional): language, timezone

2) destinations
Purpose: Telegram chats/channels where updates are published.
Key fields
1) destinationId: Telegram chat id (unique)
2) type: privatechat | group | supergroup | channel
3) title (cached)
4) enabled: boolean
5) settings
   1) minViews (default 3000)
   2) postIntervalSeconds (rate throttling per destination)
   3) includeKeywords (array)
   4) excludeKeywords (array)
   5) blockedDomains (array)
   6) templateMode (e.g., compact | detailed)
6) createdAt, updatedAt

3) sources
Purpose: Tracked X.com accounts.
Key fields
1) sourceId (unique, internal)
2) xHandle (unique)
3) displayName (cached)
4) enabled: boolean
5) tags (e.g., influencer, news, project)
6) createdAt, updatedAt

4) subscriptions
Purpose: Mapping from sources to destinations (which destinations receive which sources).
Key fields
1) destinationId
2) sourceId
3) enabled: boolean
4) perSubscriptionOverrides (optional)
   1) minViews override
   2) include/exclude keywords override
5) createdAt, updatedAt
Indexes: compound unique on (destinationId, sourceId)

5) ingestionstate
Purpose: Track polling progress per source to avoid reprocessing.
Key fields
1) sourceId (unique)
2) lastSeenPostId or lastSeenTimestamp (depending on provider)
3) lastRunAt
4) lastSuccessAt
5) failureCount, lastError

6) posts
Purpose: Store normalized posts and eligibility decisions for auditing and dedup.
Key fields
1) xPostId (unique across all)
2) sourceId
3) url
4) text (truncated or full, depending on storage policy)
5) createdAt (post time)
6) metrics
   1) views
   2) likes
   3) reposts
7) mediaRefs (optional): array of media urls/ids if available
8) ingestedAt
Indexes: xPostId unique, sourceId+createdAt

7) deliveries
Purpose: Track what was sent to which destination to prevent duplicates.
Key fields
1) xPostId
2) destinationId
3) telegramMessageId (if sent)
4) status: sent | skipped | failed
5) reason (if skipped/failed): belowthreshold, keywordfiltered, duplicate, error
6) sentAt
Indexes: compound unique on (xPostId, destinationId)

8) auditlogs
Purpose: Admin actions and configuration changes.
Key fields
1) actorTelegramUserId
2) actionType
3) entityType, entityId
4) before, after (optional snapshots)
5) createdAt

Retention policies
1) posts: retain 30-180 days depending on storage and audit needs.
2) deliveries: retain longer for dedup (e.g., 180-365 days) with indexes for fast lookups.
3) auditlogs: retain according to operational requirements.
planSections.keyFlows:
1) Initial setup (admin)
1) Admin starts bot and is recognized as admin via a configured allowlist (environment-based) or a one-time manual approval process stored in MongoDB.
2) Admin runs /setup to create the first destination and confirm the bot has permission to post.
3) Admin adds sources via /sourceadd and subscribes destinations via /subscribe.
4) Admin sets thresholds and filters globally and per destination.

2) Ingestion cycle (polling)
1) Scheduler triggers a cycle every configured interval.
2) For each enabled source:
   1) Fetch latest posts since ingestionstate cursor.
   2) Normalize items; store/merge into posts.
   3) Update ingestionstate on success.
3) For each candidate post:
   1) Determine interested destinations via subscriptions.
   2) For each destination:
      1) Check deliveries for dedup.
      2) Apply eligibility rules: view threshold (minViews), keyword filters, blocked domains.
      3) If eligible, create a pending send task in an in-memory buffer.

3) Publishing
1) Send tasks are processed with strict concurrency limits (global and per destination).
2) For each send:
   1) Render message per destination template.
   2) Send via grammY to the destination.
   3) Record delivery with telegramMessageId and status sent.
3) On failure:
   1) Record delivery status failed with reason.
   2) Apply retry policy for transient errors with capped attempts in-memory; avoid infinite retries across restarts.

4) Manual pull by users
1) /latest returns the most recent eligible posts for a destination (or via DM) without posting them to channels, depending on permissions.
2) /search (optional) allows admins to query stored posts by handle/keyword/time window for debugging.

5) Moderation and safety controls
1) /pause stops publishing but can continue ingestion (configurable) to keep state warm.
2) /resume re-enables publishing.
3) /blockkeyword and /blockdomain immediately prevent future deliveries matching these rules.
4) /sourcedisable quickly stops monitoring a problematic account.

6) Restart and recovery
1) On restart, bot loads configuration from MongoDB.
2) Scheduler resumes ingestion using stored cursors.
3) Dedup continues to work because deliveries are persisted.
4) If MongoDB is temporarily unavailable, bot enters a safe mode: no publishing, only minimal status reporting, and retries until storage returns.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and structure
1) Node.js (ESM) single-process service.
2) grammY for Telegram bot handling, including command routing, middleware, and message sending.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for configuration, state, deduplication, and audit logs.
2) In-memory maps for ephemeral runtime concerns only (in-flight tasks, short-lived caches, rate-limit counters), always safe to lose.

External integrations
1) X.com data access via a single adapter module that abstracts the chosen retrieval method.
2) Object storage or simple filesystem storage only if media caching or archival is required (optional). If used, store only derived artifacts and keep source-of-truth in MongoDB.

Operations
1) Environment variables for secrets and configuration, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.
2) Structured logging to stdout/stderr.
3) A simple health endpoint is optional if the environment supports HTTP within the same Node.js process; otherwise, use /status for health visibility.

Deployment
1) Deploy as one Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Configure process restart on crash at the hosting layer.
3) Use a single MongoDB instance/cluster already integrated with the project.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability and correctness
1) At-least-once ingestion, exactly-once delivery per destination achieved via deliveries unique index (xPostId + destinationId).
2) Safe restarts: ingestion cursors and dedup history are persisted.
3) Backpressure: bounded in-memory send queue; if exceeded, the bot skips or delays low-priority sends and reports via /status.

Rate limiting and throughput
1) Apply conservative per-destination message throttling and a global concurrency cap.
2) X.com fetching uses a per-source schedule and caps requests per cycle.
3) Use incremental polling with cursors to reduce load.

Security
1) Admin access control: only approved Telegram user ids can change configuration.
2) Secret management via environment variables.
3) Input validation for commands to prevent misconfiguration.

Observability
1) Log each ingestion cycle summary: sources processed, candidates found, eligible count, sent count, skipped reasons.
2) Log each failure with source, destination, error category.
3) /status provides: enabled sources, enabled destinations, last run timestamps, last error, queue depth, and current thresholds.

Maintainability
1) Clear modular boundaries: Telegram interface, ingestion adapter, eligibility engine, publisher, persistence layer.
2) Provider adapter designed to be replaceable without changes to core logic.

Compliance and content considerations
1) Respect the data provider’s terms and applicable restrictions.
2) Provide attribution and link back to the original post.
3) Offer an admin-controlled denylist for accounts and keywords to reduce scams/spam.

Testing strategy (high level)
1) Unit-test eligibility logic and formatting using stored fixtures.
2) Integration-test MongoDB persistence and dedup behavior.
3) End-to-end smoke test in a private Telegram chat before enabling channel posting.

DOCS.md.content:
This Telegram bot monitors selected X accounts and reposts qualifying posts into Telegram when the post has enough views.

How it works
1) You add one or more X accounts (handles).
2) You mark one or more Telegram chats as destinations.
3) The bot polls X periodically.
4) If a new post meets the minimum views threshold (default 3000) and passes keyword filters, it gets forwarded to all destinations.
5) The bot deduplicates per destination so the same X post is never sent twice to the same chat.

Public commands
1) /start
Shows what the bot does and quick setup guidance.

2) /help
Shows the full command list and troubleshooting.

3) /status
Shows the bot status (polling interval, number of accounts, number of destinations, last poll time, whether X integration is enabled).

Admin commands
Admin commands require your Telegram user ID to be listed in ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS.

1) /addaccount <handleOrUrl>
Adds an X account to monitor.
Example: /addaccount @binance
Example: /addaccount https://x.com/binance

2) /removeaccount <handleOrUrl>
Removes an X account.

3) /accounts
Lists monitored accounts.

4) /setdest
Run this in the chat you want to receive updates. The bot will save the current chat as a destination.

5) /removedest
Run this in a destination chat to remove it.

6) /dests
Lists destination chats.

7) /setthreshold <number>
Sets the minimum views threshold. It is always clamped to at least 3000.

8) /setkeywords include <comma-separated>
Sets include keywords. If include keywords are present, a post must contain at least one.
Example: /setkeywords include btc,eth,sol

9) /setkeywords exclude <comma-separated>
Sets exclude keywords. If a post contains any excluded keyword, it is skipped.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Required. Telegram token from BotFather.

2) MONGODB_URI
Required. Mongo connection string.

3) COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT
Optional but required for polling X. If missing, the bot still starts but polling is skipped.

4) COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY
Optional but required for polling X.

5) ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS
Required for admin commands. Comma-separated Telegram user IDs.
If missing, the bot will report that no admins are configured.

6) POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS
Optional. Defaults to 120.

Collections used (MongoDB)
1) settings
Stores threshold and keyword filters.

2) accounts
Monitored X accounts.

3) destinations
Telegram chats where updates are posted. The bot can auto-disable a destination after repeated send failures.

4) sent_posts
Deduplication records keyed by (destinationChatId, xPostId).

5) last_seen
Per-account cursor to reduce reprocessing.

Troubleshooting
1) The bot is not reposting anything
Check /status. If X integration is disabled, you need COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY.
Also ensure you have at least one account and at least one destination.

2) Admin commands say no admins configured
Set ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS to your Telegram numeric user ID (comma-separated if multiple), then redeploy.

3) Messages fail to send to a destination
The bot will log send errors and may auto-disable the destination after several failures. Re-add the destination using /setdest after fixing permissions.

README.md.content:
Telegram bot that monitors configured X accounts and reposts qualifying posts to Telegram when they have enough views.

Setup
1) Create a bot with BotFather and set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN.
2) Create a MongoDB database and set MONGODB_URI.
3) Configure the CookMyBots X integration and set COOKMYBOTS_X_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY.
4) Set ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS to your Telegram numeric user id.
5) Start the bot.

Run
1) npm run dev
2) npm start

Basic usage
1) In the destination chat, run /setdest
2) In any chat where you are admin, run /addaccount <handle>
3) Wait for polling, or check /status

Notes
This bot uses long polling for Telegram updates and runs in a single Node.js process.

// This is injected at generation-time so the bot can describe itself in plain chat.
export const BOT_PROMPT = "Build a Telegram bot (grammY, Node.js ES modules) that monitors crypto-related updates from x.com accounts and reposts qualifying posts into Telegram when the post has at least 3,000 views.

Core behavior:
- The bot runs a polling loop that periodically checks each configured X account for recent posts.
- For each candidate post, only forward it if view count (impressions) is >= 3000 at the time of evaluation.
- Deduplicate: never send the same X post more than once per destination chat (store sent post IDs in DB).
- Compose a Telegram message containing: account display name/handle, a trimmed text excerpt, view count, and a direct link to the post.

Commands (must include /start and /help):
- /start: explain purpose, 3000-view rule, and how to configure (admin commands).
- /help: full command list, configuration notes, and troubleshooting when X integration env vars are not set.
- Admin-only command group (only allow users whose Telegram user ID is in ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS; if missing, default to allowing only the first admin added via env-less mode = nobody, and clearly report 'no admins configured'):
  - /addaccount <handleOrUrl>: add an X account to monitor.
  - /removeaccount <handleOrUrl>: remove monitored account.
  - /accounts: list monitored accounts.
  - /setdest (in a chat): set current chat as a destination for updates.
  - /removedest (in a chat): remove current chat from destinations.
  - /dests: list destination chats.
  - /setthreshold <number>: set minimum views; validate integer and clamp to minimum 3000.
  - /setkeywords include <comma-separated> and /setkeywords exclude <comma-separated>: manage optional keyword filters.
  - /status: show bot status including polling interval, number of accounts, number of destinations, whether X integration is enabled (endpoint/key present), and last poll time.

Data persistence (MongoDB required):
- Connect using MONGODB_URI; log connect success/failure.
- Collections and suggested documents:
  - settings: si";

aiMessages.content: Create a telegram bot that shares crypto updates from x.com, the updates can be from any account so far as it has over 3k views.

Implemented the Telegram repost bot with MongoDB persistence, admin commands for accounts/destinations/threshold/keywords, an X client that calls CookMyBots X Proxy, and a safe polling loop that deduplicates per destination and skips when X integration is not configured. Added startup diagnostics, DOCS.md, README.md, and a .env.sample with the required keys.

Files touched (20): .env.sample, DOCS.md, README.md, package.json, project.json, src/bot.js…
Validation: ✅ project passes syntax/basic checks after this edit.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Crypto Updates Bot (X.com view-threshold aggregator)

Summary:
A production-ready Telegram bot that curates and forwards crypto-related updates from X.com into Telegram chats/channels. The bot ingests posts from a configurable set of X.com accounts, applies a minimum popularity threshold (over 3k views), optionally performs basic filtering/deduplication, and publishes formatted updates on a schedule or near-real-time.

The system runs as a single Node.js (ESM) process using grammY for Telegram integration, with MongoDB for persistence. It supports admin workflows to manage tracked accounts, destination chats, filters, and posting cadence; and includes guardrails for rate limits, deduplication, operational monitoring, and safe failure modes.

Product goals:
Primary outcomes
1) Deliver timely crypto updates into Telegram with minimal noise by requiring posts to exceed a view threshold (default: 3,000 views).
2) Allow admins to configure which X.com accounts are monitored and where updates are published (private groups, public channels, or direct messages).
3) Ensure updates are reliably deduplicated and posted with consistent formatting and attribution.
4) Provide operational controls: pause/resume, backfill window, posting frequency, per-account toggles, and basic keyword filtering.

Target users and roles
1) Admins (operators): configure sources, destinations, filters, and moderation settings; manage allowlists/denylists; review status.
2) Readers: consume updates in Telegram; optionally request latest items on demand.

Success criteria
1) High signal-to-noise: most forwarded posts are relevant and meet the view threshold.
2) Low duplication: the same X post is not repeatedly forwarded.
3) Operational stability: bot continues to function under API limits and transient errors, with clear status reporting.

Non-goals (initial version)
1) Complex NLP summarization, sentiment, or trading signals.
2) Multi-process scaling or external queues.
3) Full-text search across all historical posts (unless later added via MongoDB indexes).

High-level architecture:
High-level components (single Node.js service)
1) Telegram Bot Interface (grammY)
   1) Handles commands, admin configuration flows, and status queries.
   2) Sends messages to configured Telegram destinations.

2) X.com Ingestion Layer
   1) Responsible for retrieving candidate posts from configured accounts.
   2) Normalizes post metadata into an internal representation (id, author, text, url, createdat, viewcount if available, media references).
   3) Runs on a schedule (polling) and supports a controlled backfill window.

3) Filtering and Eligibility Engine
   1) Applies global and per-destination filters (minimum view threshold, keyword include/exclude, language constraints, allow/deny accounts).
   2) Enforces deduplication using a persistent record of posted X ids per destination.
   3) Applies content safety policies (e.g., block terms or domains) as configured.

4) Publishing and Formatting
   1) Composes Telegram-friendly messages (author, truncated text, link, view count, timestamp).
   2) Supports destination-specific templates (e.g., include hashtags, add source label).
   3) Handles media attachments if desired (optional, depends on ingestion metadata availability).

5) Persistence (MongoDB)
   1) Stores configuration, tracked accounts, destinations, posting state (cursors/last seen), dedup history, and audit logs.

6) Scheduler and Rate Control
   1) A simple internal scheduler runs periodic ingestion cycles.
   2) Concurrency and throughput limits prevent hitting Telegram and X rate limits.

7) Observability and Operations
   1) Structured logs for ingestion cycles, posting decisions, and errors.
   2) Health indicators (last successful run, backlog size, last posted time).
   3) Admin-facing /status that reflects current state and recent failures.

Execution model
1) Poll-based ingestion: every N seconds/minutes, fetch recent posts from each tracked account.
2) For each candidate post: compute eligibility, check dedup, then enqueue for sending within the same process using an in-memory buffer with strict concurrency limits.
3) Persist a minimal state after each cycle so restarts do not repost old items.

External dependency note
1) Accessing X.com content must be done via an approved method available to the project (e.g., an API integration or permitted fetching mechanism). The architecture treats it as an external provider and isolates it behind a single adapter so it can be swapped without impacting bot logic.
2) View-count availability depends on the provider. If view count is not available, the system can operate in a degraded mode using alternative signals (likes/retweets) or skip eligibility checks; this is configurable and clearly surfaced in /status.

Data model:
MongoDB collections (suggested)

1) users
Purpose: Telegram user records for admins/operators and optionally readers.
Key fields
1) telegramUserId (unique)
2) role: admin | viewer
3) createdAt, lastSeenAt
4) preferences (optional): language, timezone

2) destinations
Purpose: Telegram chats/channels where updates are published.
Key fields
1) destinationId: Telegram chat id (unique)
2) type: privatechat | group | supergroup | channel
3) title (cached)
4) enabled: boolean
5) settings
   1) minViews (default 3000)
   2) postIntervalSeconds (rate throttling per destination)
   3) includeKeywords (array)
   4) excludeKeywords (array)
   5) blockedDomains (array)
   6) templateMode (e.g., compact | detailed)
6) createdAt, updatedAt

3) sources
Purpose: Tracked X.com accounts.
Key fields
1) sourceId (unique, internal)
2) xHandle (unique)
3) displayName (cached)
4) enabled: boolean
5) tags (e.g., influencer, news, project)
6) createdAt, updatedAt

4) subscriptions
Purpose: Mapping from sources to destinations (which destinations receive which sources).
Key fields
1) destinationId
2) sourceId
3) enabled: boolean
4) perSubscriptionOverrides (optional)
   1) minViews override
   2) include/exclude keywords override
5) createdAt, updatedAt
Indexes: compound unique on (destinationId, sourceId)

5) ingestionstate
Purpose: Track polling progress per source to avoid reprocessing.
Key fields
1) sourceId (unique)
2) lastSeenPostId or lastSeenTimestamp (depending on provider)
3) lastRunAt
4) lastSuccessAt
5) failureCount, lastError

6) posts
Purpose: Store normalized posts and eligibility decisions for auditing and dedup.
Key fields
1) xPostId (unique across all)
2) sourceId
3) url
4) text (truncated or full, depending on storage policy)
5) createdAt (post time)
6) metrics
   1) views
   2) likes
   3) reposts
7) mediaRefs (optional): array of media urls/ids if available
8) ingestedAt
Indexes: xPostId unique, sourceId+createdAt

7) deliveries
Purpose: Track what was sent to which destination to prevent duplicates.
Key fields
1) xPostId
2) destinationId
3) telegramMessageId (if sent)
4) status: sent | skipped | failed
5) reason (if skipped/failed): belowthreshold, keywordfiltered, duplicate, error
6) sentAt
Indexes: compound unique on (xPostId, destinationId)

8) auditlogs
Purpose: Admin actions and configuration changes.
Key fields
1) actorTelegramUserId
2) actionType
3) entityType, entityId
4) before, after (optional snapshots)
5) createdAt

Retention policies
1) posts: retain 30-180 days depending on storage and audit needs.
2) deliveries: retain longer for dedup (e.g., 180-365 days) with indexes for fast lookups.
3) auditlogs: retain according to operational requirements.

Key flows:
1) Initial setup (admin)
1) Admin starts bot and is recognized as admin via a configured allowlist (environment-based) or a one-time manual approval process stored in MongoDB.
2) Admin runs /setup to create the first destination and confirm the bot has permission to post.
3) Admin adds sources via /sourceadd and subscribes destinations via /subscribe.
4) Admin sets thresholds and filters globally and per destination.

2) Ingestion cycle (polling)
1) Scheduler triggers a cycle every configured interval.
2) For each enabled source:
   1) Fetch latest posts since ingestionstate cursor.
   2) Normalize items; store/merge into posts.
   3) Update ingestionstate on success.
3) For each candidate post:
   1) Determine interested destinations via subscriptions.
   2) For each destination:
      1) Check deliveries for dedup.
      2) Apply eligibility rules: view threshold (minViews), keyword filters, blocked domains.
      3) If eligible, create a pending send task in an in-memory buffer.

3) Publishing
1) Send tasks are processed with strict concurrency limits (global and per destination).
2) For each send:
   1) Render message per destination template.
   2) Send via grammY to the destination.
   3) Record delivery with telegramMessageId and status sent.
3) On failure:
   1) Record delivery status failed with reason.
   2) Apply retry policy for transient errors with capped attempts in-memory; avoid infinite retries across restarts.

4) Manual pull by users
1) /latest returns the most recent eligible posts for a destination (or via DM) without posting them to channels, depending on permissions.
2) /search (optional) allows admins to query stored posts by handle/keyword/time window for debugging.

5) Moderation and safety controls
1) /pause stops publishing but can continue ingestion (configurable) to keep state warm.
2) /resume re-enables publishing.
3) /blockkeyword and /blockdomain immediately prevent future deliveries matching these rules.
4) /sourcedisable quickly stops monitoring a problematic account.

6) Restart and recovery
1) On restart, bot loads configuration from MongoDB.
2) Scheduler resumes ingestion using stored cursors.
3) Dedup continues to work because deliveries are persisted.
4) If MongoDB is temporarily unavailable, bot enters a safe mode: no publishing, only minimal status reporting, and retries until storage returns.

Tech stack:
Runtime and structure
1) Node.js (ESM) single-process service.
2) grammY for Telegram bot handling, including command routing, middleware, and message sending.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for configuration, state, deduplication, and audit logs.
2) In-memory maps for ephemeral runtime concerns only (in-flight tasks, short-lived caches, rate-limit counters), always safe to lose.

External integrations
1) X.com data access via a single adapter module that abstracts the chosen retrieval method.
2) Object storage or simple filesystem storage only if media caching or archival is required (optional). If used, store only derived artifacts and keep source-of-truth in MongoDB.

Operations
1) Environment variables for secrets and configuration, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN.
2) Structured logging to stdout/stderr.
3) A simple health endpoint is optional if the environment supports HTTP within the same Node.js process; otherwise, use /status for health visibility.

Deployment
1) Deploy as one Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Configure process restart on crash at the hosting layer.
3) Use a single MongoDB instance/cluster already integrated with the project.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) At-least-once ingestion, exactly-once delivery per destination achieved via deliveries unique index (xPostId + destinationId).
2) Safe restarts: ingestion cursors and dedup history are persisted.
3) Backpressure: bounded in-memory send queue; if exceeded, the bot skips or delays low-priority sends and reports via /status.

Rate limiting and throughput
1) Apply conservative per-destination message throttling and a global concurrency cap.
2) X.com fetching uses a per-source schedule and caps requests per cycle.
3) Use incremental polling with cursors to reduce load.

Security
1) Admin access control: only approved Telegram user ids can change configuration.
2) Secret management via environment variables.
3) Input validation for commands to prevent misconfiguration.

Observability
1) Log each ingestion cycle summary: sources processed, candidates found, eligible count, sent count, skipped reasons.
2) Log each failure with source, destination, error category.
3) /status provides: enabled sources, enabled destinations, last run timestamps, last error, queue depth, and current thresholds.

Maintainability
1) Clear modular boundaries: Telegram interface, ingestion adapter, eligibility engine, publisher, persistence layer.
2) Provider adapter designed to be replaceable without changes to core logic.

Compliance and content considerations
1) Respect the data provider’s terms and applicable restrictions.
2) Provide attribution and link back to the original post.
3) Offer an admin-controlled denylist for accounts and keywords to reduce scams/spam.

Testing strategy (high level)
1) Unit-test eligibility logic and formatting using stored fixtures.
2) Integration-test MongoDB persistence and dedup behavior.
3) End-to-end smoke test in a private Telegram chat before enabling channel posting.

Bot commands:
• /start — Initialize the bot and show basic help plus current status for the user. (usage: /start) [general]
• /help — Show available commands and usage, tailored to admin vs non-admin. (usage: /help) [general]
• /status — Show operational status: sources, destinations, last ingestion run, last error, queue depth, and current thresholds. (usage: /status) [operations]
• /setup — Guide an admin through initial setup: register a destination chat/channel and default settings. (usage: /setup) [admin]
• /destadd — Register the current chat as a destination for postings (admin only). (usage: /destadd) [admin]
• /destlist — List all configured destinations and key settings. (usage: /destlist) [admin]
• /destenable — Enable publishing to a destination. (usage: /destenable <destinationId>) [admin]
• /destdisable — Disable publishing to a destination without deleting configuration. (usage: /destdisable <destinationId>) [admin]
• /sourceadd — Add an X.com account to monitor. (usage: /sourceadd <xHandle>) [admin]
• /sourcelist — List monitored X.com accounts and whether they are enabled. (usage: /sourcelist) [admin]
• /sourceenable — Enable monitoring for an account. (usage: /sourceenable <xHandle>) [admin]
• /sourcedisable — Disable monitoring for an account. (usage: /sourcedisable <xHandle>) [admin]
• /subscribe — Subscribe a destination to a source (start forwarding eligible posts). (usage: /subscribe <destinationId> <xHandle>) [admin]
• /unsubscribe — Remove a subscription between destination and source. (usage: /unsubscribe <destinationId> <xHandle>) [admin]
• /setminviews — Set the minimum view threshold globally or per destination (default 3000). (usage: /setminviews <views> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /setinterval — Set ingestion polling interval and/or per-destination posting throttle. (usage: /setinterval <seconds> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /filterinclude — Set or add include keywords for eligibility (global or per destination). (usage: /filterinclude <keyword1,keyword2,...> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /filterexclude — Set or add exclude keywords for eligibility (global or per destination). (usage: /filterexclude <keyword1,keyword2,...> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /blockdomain — Block posts containing links to specific domains (global or per destination). (usage: /blockdomain <domain> [destinationId]) [admin]
• /pause — Pause publishing (optionally continue ingestion) for maintenance or incident response. (usage: /pause) [operations]
• /resume — Resume publishing after a pause. (usage: /resume) [operations]
• /latest — Show the most recent eligible posts for a destination or source without altering delivery state (useful for spot checks). (usage: /latest [xHandle] [limit]) [general]
• /backfill — Trigger a controlled backfill for the last N hours for one source or all sources (admin only). (usage: /backfill <hours> [xHandle]) [admin]

state.title: Telegram Crypto Updates Bot

envVarDocs.title: Telegram Bot Token
envVarDocs.description: Token for your Telegram bot from BotFather.
envVarDocs.usage: Used by grammY to connect to Telegram.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Create a bot with BotFather and copy the token.

envVarDocs.title: MongoDB Connection String
envVarDocs.description: MongoDB URI used for settings, accounts, destinations, cursors, and deduplication.
envVarDocs.usage: The bot connects once at startup and reuses the connection.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Create a MongoDB database (Atlas or self-hosted) and copy the connection string.

envVarDocs.title: CookMyBots X Proxy Endpoint
envVarDocs.description: Base URL for CookMyBots X proxy. If missing, X polling is disabled but the bot still runs.
envVarDocs.usage: Used by src/services/xClient.js for all X API calls via /proxy.
envVarDocs.howToGet: From your CookMyBots project integration settings.

envVarDocs.title: CookMyBots X Proxy Key
envVarDocs.description: Bearer key for the CookMyBots X proxy. If missing, X polling is disabled.
envVarDocs.usage: Sent as Authorization: Bearer COOKMYBOTS_X_KEY to the proxy.
envVarDocs.howToGet: From your CookMyBots project integration settings.

envVarDocs.title: Admin Telegram User IDs
envVarDocs.description: Comma-separated list of Telegram numeric user IDs allowed to run admin commands.
envVarDocs.usage: Checked by src/lib/admin.js. If not set, admin commands are blocked with a clear message.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Use a Telegram user info bot or check your Telegram client logs to find your numeric user ID.

envVarDocs.title: Polling Interval Seconds
envVarDocs.description: How often the bot polls X for new posts.
envVarDocs.usage: Used by the poller loop. Defaults to 120 if missing or invalid.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Set it in your deployment environment.

commandDocs.description: Explains what the bot does and how admins configure accounts and destinations.
examples: User: /start → Bot: explains the 3000-view rule and setup steps
commandDocs.notes: If X integration is not configured, /start mentions it.

commandDocs.description: Shows the full command list plus troubleshooting for missing X integration or admin config.
examples: User: /help → Bot: lists commands and configuration notes
commandDocs.notes: Admin commands require ADMIN_TELEGRAM_USER_IDS.

commandDocs.description: Shows bot status: polling interval, configured accounts/destinations, X integration enabled, and last poll time.
examples: User: /status → Bot: shows counts, threshold, and last poll timestamp
commandDocs.notes: If X integration is disabled, status will show it.

commandDocs.description: Admin-only. Adds an X account to monitor.
examples: Admin: /addaccount @binance
Admin: /addaccount https://x.com/binance
commandDocs.notes: Handles are normalized to lowercase without @.

commandDocs.description: Admin-only. Removes a monitored X account.
examples: Admin: /removeaccount @binance
commandDocs.notes: Also clears its last_seen cursor.

commandDocs.description: Admin-only. Lists monitored X accounts.
examples: Admin: /accounts → Bot: Accounts: @binance, @coinbase

commandDocs.description: Admin-only. Marks the current Telegram chat as a destination for reposts.
examples: Admin (in a channel/group): /setdest → Bot: This chat is now a destination
commandDocs.notes: Make sure the bot has permission to post in the chat.

commandDocs.description: Admin-only. Removes the current chat from destinations.
examples: Admin (in a destination chat): /removedest → Bot: removed

commandDocs.description: Admin-only. Lists destination chats.
examples: Admin: /dests → Bot: Destinations: My Group (123...), My Channel (456...)
commandDocs.notes: Destinations can be auto-disabled after repeated send failures.

commandDocs.description: Admin-only. Sets the minimum views threshold; clamped to at least 3000.
examples: Admin: /setthreshold 5000 → Bot: Threshold set to 5000 views

commandDocs.description: Admin-only. Sets include or exclude keyword filters.
examples: Admin: /setkeywords include btc,eth,sol
Admin: /setkeywords exclude airdrop,referral
commandDocs.notes: If include list is non-empty, the post must match at least one include keyword.
